When you write a function, the single most useful rule I follow is: a function should do one thing, and its name should tell you what that thing is. If you find yourself adding "and" to the name, split it. Another guideline: prefer returning early over deep nesting. Every level of indentation is a level of cognitive load. Keep your error handling close to where the error can happen, not bundled at the bottom. And one I learned the hard way: never catch an exception you don't know how to handle. Swallowing errors is how silent bugs are born. Finally, write the test first. If you can't describe the behavior as a test, you don't understand it well enough to implement it yet.
